content:
3 min read
5 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I’ve spent a long time annoying my boss to allow me to upgrade our codebase to support SSR. He finally relented and I spend most of 2025 working through a seven year old code base dragging it into the future with standalone components, better routing configuration and adding Signals to solve flaky tests and unresponsive changes.
The first problem came when a developer on our team updated our package-lock.json file with a new version of a few library and all my work came tumbling down. It took me three or four full days to figure out what happened. Angular 18 had a new patch version that changed how SSR worked. You needed to pass an extra argument to the bootstrapping code. This was not listed as a breaking change. The only reason I figured this out was looking at patch notes on Github. Thank the maker they list these things because this would happen again.
The second problem came when I updated the Patch version due to a security finding and SSR stopped working. A change slated for Angular 21 made it’s way into 20. It forces you to list the allowed domains that are allowed to render pages on the server. This wasn’t listed as a breaking change.
The third problem came when I had to update our package-lock.json file and once again SSR “kinda” stopped working. This time the page rendered on the server but didn’t store the the data request it made. Again this took another two or three days to track down. I was convenced I broke something because a lot of things changed in that pull request. After stepping away and remembering that a patch change broke SSR I decided to check the patch notes and low-and-behold the patch version changed and introduced a breaking change for the way data requests are stored. If your request uses the “withCredentials” header it wasn’t stored.
I love Angular. I’ve been a big supporter since 1.x. I stuck with them during the uprising of 2.x and looked forward to every new feature they added. It took me wwwaaayyyy to long to figure out how to use RxJs but once I did I could see the gears of the universe turning in the sky. I’ve defended it’s slow performance and spent the time to transform it from a sedan to a F1 race car.
All this to say I’m really tired of losing weeks of my life trying to keep a feature that doesn’t seem important to the Angular team working. SSR is a massive gain in performance and SEO results. Upper management has been thrilled with the new page load speeds and we’re continuing to make updates to the rest of our pages to make things better and faster.
I guess the answer is to pin angular to a specific version and never look forward. The problem with this is it makes upgrading to new versions even worse because you have to face all these little show-stopper bugs at once. I wish the Angular team would save these changes for Minor upgrades so it’s more clear that you’ve got some work to do.
